front: Explain the Checks-Effects-Interactions (CEI) pattern and why it prevents reentrancy.
back: CEI mandates that a function: (1) Checks — validate all preconditions and access controls; (2) Effects — update all contract state (balances, flags); (3) Interactions — make external calls last. Reentrancy works by re-entering the function before state is updated. By writing state before calling out, any re-entrant call sees the already-updated state and fails its checks. The DAO hack ($60M, 2016) happened because ETH was sent before balances were zeroed.
senior_nuance: CEI alone is insufficient against cross-function and cross-contract reentrancy. A nonReentrant modifier is the belt-and-suspenders approach. ReentrancyGuard costs ~2300 gas per call due to the SSTORE/SLOAD pair on the lock slot.
quote_to_say: Check, update state, then call out — in that order, always.

front: What is the difference between single-function, cross-function, and cross-contract reentrancy? Give an example of each attack vector.
back: Single-function: attacker re-enters the same function (classic The DAO). Cross-function: attacker calls a different function in the same contract that shares state with the vulnerable one (e.g., withdraw() → transfer() both reference balances). Cross-contract: two contracts share state; reentrancy in contract A calls B which calls back into A. The Curve reentrancy (2023) was a cross-contract variant triggered by a Vyper compiler bug in the read-only reentrancy guard for view functions.
senior_nuance: Read-only reentrancy is subtle: view functions that read state mid-update return stale data. A price oracle reading a pool balance during a reentrancy can be manipulated without draining the pool directly.
quote_to_say: Read-only reentrancy is the silent killer — your view function can be the attack vector.

front: How did integer overflow/underflow work in Solidity before 0.8, and what changed in 0.8?
back: Before 0.8, arithmetic silently wrapped around at type boundaries (uint256 max + 1 = 0). Classic exploit: if (balances[msg.sender] - amount >= 0) — with uint256, subtracting more than the balance wraps to a huge number, bypassing the check. SafeMath (OpenZeppelin) was the standard mitigation: it reverts on overflow/underflow. Solidity 0.8 made checked arithmetic the default — overflow/underflow reverts automatically. Use unchecked {} block only for gas optimization when you have a mathematical proof the wrap cannot occur.
senior_nuance: unchecked {} in 0.8 is not unsafe by default — it is a deliberate optimization. The risk is developers adding it cargo-cult style. Always audit every unchecked block.
quote_to_say: Pre-0.8: uint arithmetic wraps. 0.8+: it reverts. unchecked is opt-in danger.

front: Explain the delegatecall storage layout collision vulnerability. How did it cause the Parity multisig hack?
back: delegatecall executes code from another contract but reads/writes storage of the calling contract using the callee's slot layout. If slot indices differ between caller and callee, writes land in wrong slots. Parity Multisig (2017, ~$30M): the wallet library had an initWallet() function that was not access-controlled. An attacker called it directly on the library contract (not a wallet), making themselves the owner. They then called kill(), which selfdestruct'd the library. All wallets delegating to it became permanently bricked — $154M frozen. A second bug: the proxy storage layout did not match the library, so ownership slots collided.
senior_nuance: EIP-1967 standardized proxy storage slots using hashed keys (keccak256('eip1967.proxy.implementation') - 1) to avoid collision with normal slot layout. Always use EIP-1967 slots in proxies.
quote_to_say: delegatecall runs their code on your storage — layout mismatch is a footgun.

front: Why is tx.origin dangerous for authentication, and when is it safe to use?
back: tx.origin is the original EOA that started the transaction chain — it does not change through contract calls. Using it for auth means any intermediate contract can impersonate the user: malicious contract B calls your contract C, C sees tx.origin == user and grants permission. msg.sender is always the immediate caller. tx.origin is safe only for: (1) blocking contract callers (require(tx.origin == msg.sender)) — useful for preventing flash loan attacks; (2) fee refund accounting in meta-transactions. Never use for ownership checks.
senior_nuance: EIP-4337 Account Abstraction breaks the tx.origin == EOA assumption — smart contract wallets can be the initiator. Avoid tx.origin entirely in ERC-4337 context.
quote_to_say: msg.sender = who called you. tx.origin = who started the chain. Use msg.sender for auth.

front: Compare Transparent Proxy, UUPS, and Beacon Proxy patterns. What are the security tradeoffs?
back: Transparent Proxy (OpenZeppelin v3): admin calls go to proxy, user calls delegate. Prevents function selector clashing. Admin cannot call implementation functions. Cost: extra SLOAD per call to check admin. UUPS (EIP-1822): upgrade logic lives in the implementation, not the proxy. Cheaper calls (no admin check on every call). Risk: if you deploy a broken implementation without upgradeToAndCall, you brick the proxy — there is no fallback upgrade path. Beacon Proxy: multiple proxies share one beacon pointing to one implementation. One upgrade updates all. Best for factory patterns with many identical contracts.
senior_nuance: UUPS is preferred for gas but requires the implementation to include the upgrade function. Forgetting it (or having it in an unreachable code path) permanently freezes the contract. Always test upgrade paths in a fork before deploying.
quote_to_say: UUPS is cheaper but riskier — the upgrade guard must be in the implementation you can reach.

front: What is front-running in EVM blockchains? Name three on-chain mitigations.
back: Front-running: an attacker observes a pending tx in the mempool and submits a competing tx with higher gas, guaranteed to execute first. Common in DEX trades (sandwich), NFT mints, and auction bids. Mitigations: (1) Commit-reveal scheme — commit hash(value, nonce) first, reveal later; (2) Slippage/deadline parameter — trade reverts if price moves too far; (3) Private mempools (Flashbots Protect, MEV Blocker) — tx bypasses public mempool; (4) Batch auction settlement (CoW Protocol) — fill at uniform clearing price, no front-run benefit. On-chain mitigations can be combined: slippage + deadline is standard in Uniswap.
senior_nuance: Commit-reveal has a delay window that can be exploited if the committed value is guessable. Use a strong random nonce and short reveal window. For Dutch auctions, monotonically decreasing price removes the front-run incentive.
quote_to_say: Public mempool = adversarial broadcast. Design like someone will always see your tx first.

front: Explain oracle price manipulation. How does TWAP mitigate it, and what are TWAP's own weaknesses?
back: Spot price manipulation: attacker uses a flash loan to move a DEX pool price, calls a contract that reads the spot price as an oracle, profits, repays loan in the same tx. Compound 2020 ($90M liquidations): DAI price spiked on Coinbase oracle; massive liquidations followed. TWAP (Time-Weighted Average Price): averages price over N blocks. Manipulating it requires sustained capital over many blocks — economically infeasible at scale. TWAP weaknesses: (1) stale during low-liquidity periods; (2) exploitable on small chains where capital cost is low; (3) latency means it lags real price, creating arbitrage windows. Chainlink aggregates multiple sources with deviation thresholds and heartbeat checks.
senior_nuance: Using only one oracle type is a single point of failure. Robust protocols combine Chainlink (manipulation-resistant, external) with TWAP (on-chain, censorship-resistant) and use the minimum or deviation circuit breaker.
quote_to_say: Spot price = flash loan target. TWAP = time is your protection, but time is also your lag.

front: How do flash loan attacks work mechanically? What contracts are vulnerable and why?
back: Flash loans are uncollateralized loans repaid within the same tx. Atomicity: if repayment fails, the whole tx reverts — no risk to lender. Attack flow: (1) borrow huge capital; (2) manipulate on-chain state (price, votes, collateral ratios); (3) exploit target contract; (4) profit; (5) repay loan. Vulnerable patterns: spot price oracles (price moves = misvalued collateral), governance contracts with token-weighted quorum (borrow votes to pass proposal), AMMs with single-block liquidity assumptions. Beanstalk (2022, $182M): attacker flash-loaned governance tokens, passed malicious proposal in one block, drained treasury.
senior_nuance: Flash loans are not inherently bad — they enable efficient capital markets and are used for liquidations and arbitrage. Vulnerability lies in contracts that trust single-block capital as a signal of long-term commitment.
quote_to_say: Flash loans weaponize atomicity. If your contract's security scales with capital, a flash loan breaks that assumption.

front: What is ECDSA signature malleability and how does EIP-2 fix it?
back: ECDSA signatures are (r, s, v) tuples. The secp256k1 curve is symmetric: for any valid (r, s), (r, n-s) is also a valid signature for the same message and key (where n is the curve order). Pre-EIP-2, both forms recovered to the same address. An attacker could take a valid sig, flip s to n-s, and replay it as a 'different' signature — breaking uniqueness assumptions. EIP-2 (Homestead): mandated that s must be in the lower half of the curve order (s <= n/2). EIP-2098 compact encoding also enforces this. OpenZeppelin's ECDSA.recover() includes low-s enforcement and reverts on malleable sigs.
senior_nuance: Signature malleability is a problem only when you use the raw signature bytes as a replay-protection key (e.g., mapping(bytes => bool) used). Use a nonce-based scheme instead — malleability is then irrelevant even with malleable sigs.
quote_to_say: Never use raw sig bytes as a unique identifier. Use (from, nonce) — sigs are malleable.

front: What is the ERC-20 approve race condition, and how does EIP-2612 permit() eliminate it?
back: approve() race: user approves spender for 100 tokens to change to 50. Spender front-runs the second approve, spends the original 100, then spends the 50 from the new approval — spender gets 150 total. Mitigations: increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance helpers (avoid setting from nonzero to nonzero); or approve(0) first. EIP-2612 permit(): user signs an off-chain message (EIP-712 typed data) containing spender, amount, nonce, deadline. Spender calls permit() to set allowance and transferFrom() in one tx. No approve tx ever hits the mempool — no race condition, one less tx, gasless for the user.
senior_nuance: permit() shifts griefing risk: an attacker can front-run permit() by calling it first (using the sig from mempool). The spender's subsequent call fails. Mitigate by using try/catch around permit() in the spender contract — if allowance is already set, skip permit().
quote_to_say: ERC-20 approve is two txs with a race. permit() is one sig — gasless and atomic.

front: What is the difference between Ownable and AccessControl in OpenZeppelin, and when should you choose each?
back: Ownable: single owner address, transferOwnership(). Simple, minimal gas. Suitable for simple contracts with one admin. AccessControl: role-based (bytes32 role identifiers), multiple accounts per role, grantRole/revokeRole, role admins. Suitable for complex protocols with different privilege levels (pauser, upgrader, minter, governor). Key methods: hasRole(), grantRole(), revokeRole(). Roles are stored as keccak256 hashes. DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE can manage all other roles — treat it like root access.
senior_nuance: Two-step ownership transfer (Ownable2Step) is critical: the new owner must explicitly accept. Without it, a typo in the new owner address permanently loses ownership. Always use Ownable2Step or AccessControl with a governance timelock for production.
quote_to_say: Ownable is fine for prototypes. Production needs roles, timelocks, and two-step transfers.

front: Describe the Wormhole bridge hack ($326M, 2022). What class of vulnerability was it?
back: Wormhole is a cross-chain messaging bridge. The Solana side used a Solana program to verify guardian (validator) signatures on messages. The vulnerability: the program called a deprecated Solana sys_instruction sysvar to verify signatures, but did not validate that the sysvar account passed was actually the real system sysvar. An attacker passed a spoofed account that returned successful signature verification for any input — effectively bypassing all guardian signature checks. The attacker minted 120,000 wETH on Ethereum backed by a fabricated Solana deposit, then bridged them out. Root cause: missing account ownership/identity validation, a class of bug endemic to Solana's account model.
senior_nuance: In Solana programs, every account passed to an instruction must be validated: check owner, check key, check is_signer/is_writable. Never trust account data without verifying the account's identity. Anchor's #[account] macro enforces this automatically — use it.
quote_to_say: Bridges are attack surface squared — they trust two chains simultaneously. Wormhole trusted neither correctly.

front: What caused the Ronin bridge hack ($625M, 2022)? What architectural lesson does it teach?
back: Ronin is the Ethereum sidechain for Axie Infinity. It used a multisig bridge requiring 5-of-9 validator signatures to approve withdrawals. Sky Mavis (Axie developer) had been granted temporary approval to sign on behalf of Axie DAO — 4 Sky Mavis keys + 1 Axie DAO key = 5 of 9. This permission was never revoked after the temporary need ended. An attacker (later attributed to Lazarus Group, North Korea) compromised 4 Sky Mavis keys via a spear-phishing attack and used the still-valid Axie DAO key. Result: 5/9 threshold met, $625M drained over two transactions. The exploit went unnoticed for 6 days.
senior_nuance: Key management hygiene and access review schedules are as important as code. A technically perfect contract can be drained through social engineering. Geographic and organizational key distribution matters — Sky Mavis held too many keys.
quote_to_say: The code was fine. The key rotation wasn't. $625M is the price of skipping access reviews.

front: What are the security implications of selfdestruct()? How has EIP-6780 changed the behavior?
back: Pre-EIP-6780: selfdestruct() deletes all contract bytecode and storage, and force-sends its ETH balance to a target — even if the target has no fallback (bypasses receive/fallback). Attack vectors: (1) destroy a contract another depends on; (2) force-send ETH to break contracts using address(this).balance as a sentinel (e.g., require(balance == 0)). Parity: attacker called kill() on the library, destroying it and bricking 587 wallets. EIP-6780 (Dencun, 2024): selfdestruct no longer deletes code or storage unless the contract was created in the same transaction. It still sends ETH. CREATE2 + selfdestruct for address-reuse attacks now fail — code cannot be redeployed to a clean address.
senior_nuance: Never use address(this).balance as a security invariant — ETH can always be force-sent (selfdestruct, coinbase). Track balances in storage variables instead.
quote_to_say: EIP-6780 neutered selfdestruct — code persists, only ETH moves. Never assume your contract can be self-destructed post-Dencun.
